name: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act - Security Rule
description: Federal law governing the privacy and security of protected health information (PHI). The January 2025 Final Rule is the first major overhaul of the HIPAA Security Rule since 2013, strengthening requirements for risk analysis, MFA, encryption at rest, technology asset inventories, vulnerability management, and business associate oversight.

highlights: HITECH Act enacted: business associates made directly liable for Security Rule compliance
Breach Notification Rule introduced (interim final rule) with 60-day notification requirement
Enforcement significantly strengthened: tiered penalties up to $1.5M per violation category
Electronic health record (EHR) adoption incentives created meaningful use requirements
Business associates required to comply with Security Rule even without contractual obligation

changes.title: HITECH Act — Business Associates Directly Subject to HIPAA Security Rule
changes.newText: Section 13401 of the HITECH Act (42 U.S.C. § 17931) made business associates directly subject to the HIPAA Security Rule requirements that apply to covered entities. Business associates must implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for ePHI they create, receive, maintain, or transmit. Business associates are directly liable to HHS OCR for Security Rule violations independent of any business associate agreement.
changes.rationale: Prior to HITECH, business associates were only obligated to protect PHI through contractual BAAs — there was no direct regulatory liability. HITECH fundamentally changed this by making BAs directly liable, enabling OCR to investigate and penalize BAs directly. This was enacted in response to data breaches at BA organizations that faced no regulatory consequences.

changes.title: HITECH Act — Breach Notification Requirement Established
changes.newText: Section 13402 of the HITECH Act required covered entities to notify affected individuals, HHS, and (for breaches affecting 500+ individuals in a state) prominent media outlets of breaches of unsecured PHI. Notification must occur without unreasonable delay and within 60 calendar days of discovery. An interim final rule (45 CFR Parts 160 and 164) was issued in August 2009 implementing these requirements. The interim rule included a 'harm threshold' (notification only if breach poses significant risk of harm) that was later removed by the 2013 Omnibus Rule.
changes.rationale: Before HITECH, there was no statutory requirement to notify individuals or HHS of PHI breaches. Several high-profile data breaches at healthcare organizations and their business associates occurred without patient notification. HITECH created the first federal healthcare breach notification requirement.

changes.title: HITECH Act — Tiered Civil Money Penalties Established
changes.newText: Section 13410 of the HITECH Act established tiered civil money penalties: (1) unknowing violations ($100-$50K per violation, max $25K per year pre-HITECH, now max $1.5M per category per year); (2) reasonable cause ($1K-$100K); (3) willful neglect corrected ($10K-$250K); (4) willful neglect not corrected ($50K-$1.5M). State attorneys general were also authorized to bring civil actions for HIPAA violations on behalf of state residents.
changes.rationale: Pre-HITECH HIPAA penalties maxed at $100 per violation with a $25,000 annual cap per violation category, which created insufficient deterrence. Multiple covered entities suffered large-scale breaches with minimal regulatory consequences. HITECH dramatically increased penalty authority to create meaningful deterrence.

changes.title: Security Management — Risk Analysis Required for Meaningful Use EHR Attestation
changes.oldText: A covered entity must implement policies and procedures to prevent, detect, contain, and correct security violations. Risk analysis: conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of potential risks and vulnerabilities to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI.
changes.newText: A covered entity must implement policies and procedures to prevent, detect, contain, and correct security violations. Risk analysis is required, and in the context of HITECH meaningful use incentives, completion of a HIPAA Security Risk Assessment (SRA) became a required attestation for eligible providers seeking EHR incentive payments. The risk analysis must assess the potential risks and vulnerabilities to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all ePHI that the organization creates, receives, maintains, or transmits.
changes.rationale: The HITECH Act created financial incentives for EHR adoption under the Medicare and Medicaid EHR Incentive Programs (Meaningful Use). One mandatory attestation requirement was completion of a HIPAA Security Risk Assessment. This dramatically increased compliance with the risk analysis requirement as providers sought incentive payments.

changes.title: Accounting of Disclosures — EHR Disclosure Accounting Expanded
changes.oldText: An individual has a right to receive an accounting of disclosures of protected health information made by a covered entity for the six years prior to the date on which the accounting is requested.
changes.newText: An individual has a right to receive an accounting of disclosures. For covered entities using an EHR, HITECH extended the accounting of disclosures requirement to include disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations (previously excluded). For EHR-based disclosures, the accounting period extends back three years (rather than six). This requirement was controversial and full implementation was delayed pending rulemaking.
changes.rationale: HITECH required HHS to expand accounting of disclosures for EHR users to include treatment, payment, and health care operations disclosures — a significant expansion from prior HIPAA rules. This provision was intended to improve transparency for patients whose EHR data is widely shared in health information exchanges.

changes.title: Training — Security Awareness Training Made Explicit Best Practice for EHR Users
changes.oldText: A covered entity must train all members of its workforce on the policies and procedures with respect to protected health information required by this subpart and subpart D, as necessary and appropriate for the members of the workforce to carry out their functions.
changes.newText: Workforce training requirements remain unchanged in statute, but HITECH-era guidance and meaningful use criteria made security awareness training a prominent focus. CMS and ONC guidance for meaningful use attestation emphasized security training for all workforce members accessing EHR systems. The Office of the National Coordinator (ONC) published guidance on EHR security training content requirements for meaningful use compliance.
changes.rationale: Meaningful use required covered entities to have implemented HIPAA security training. While the regulatory text did not change, HITECH-era policy guidance significantly elevated the practical expectations for training programs, particularly for workforce members accessing electronic health records.

highlights: Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) made mandatory by statute — BAs now directly liable under HIPAA Security Rule
Breach Notification Rule finalized: 60-day notification required, 'harm threshold' removed
Civil Money Penalties tiered into four categories with maximums up to $1.5M per violation category per year
Marketing and fundraising restrictions tightened: prior authorization required for most treatment/payment marketing using PHI
Minimum Necessary standard strengthened and applied to all disclosures including to BAs
Research: simplified authorization for future research use and combination authorizations allowed
Right of Access: individuals may request electronic copies of PHI in ePHI format
Sale of PHI prohibited without explicit authorization
Subcontractor BAs: BAs must enter BAAs with their own subcontractors handling PHI

changes.title: Business Associate Contracts — BAs Directly Liable Under Security Rule
changes.oldText: A covered entity may permit a business associate to create, receive, maintain, or transmit electronic protected health information on the covered entity's behalf only if the covered entity obtains satisfactory assurances, in accordance with 164.314(a), that the business associate will appropriately safeguard the information.
changes.newText: A covered entity may permit a business associate to create, receive, maintain, or transmit electronic protected health information on the covered entity's behalf only if the covered entity obtains satisfactory assurances, in accordance with 164.314(a), that the business associate will appropriately safeguard the information. Business associates are directly liable for compliance with the Security Rule requirements applicable to them, regardless of whether they have a signed BAA. Business associates of business associates (subcontractors) must also have BAAs in place.
changes.rationale: The HITECH Act (2009) made business associates directly liable for HIPAA compliance by statute. The 2013 Omnibus Rule finalized this change in regulations. Prior to 2013, BAs were only contractually obligated; now they face direct enforcement and civil penalties from HHS OCR.

changes.title: Breach Notification Rule — 60-Day Notification, Harm Threshold Removed
changes.newText: Following the discovery of a breach of unsecured protected health information, a covered entity must notify each individual whose unsecured PHI has been, or is reasonably believed to have been, accessed, acquired, used, or disclosed as a result of such breach. Notification must be provided without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 calendar days after discovery of the breach. The 2009 interim rule's 'harm threshold' (requiring breach notification only when the breach posed significant risk of financial, reputational, or other harm) is replaced by a presumption that all breaches require notification unless the covered entity demonstrates low probability that PHI was compromised under a four-factor risk assessment.
changes.rationale: The 2009 HITECH interim breach notification rule included a 'harm threshold' that was widely criticized for creating a loophole allowing covered entities to avoid notification for many breaches. The 2013 Omnibus Rule replaced this with a risk assessment approach where the default is notification, with non-notification only permitted when a four-factor analysis demonstrates low probability of compromise.

changes.title: Breach Notification — Tiered Civil Money Penalties ($100 to $1.5M per category)
changes.newText: Civil Money Penalties (CMPs) for HIPAA violations are tiered into four categories based on culpability: (1) Unknowing violation: $100-$50,000 per violation; (2) Reasonable cause: $1,000-$100,000 per violation; (3) Willful neglect, corrected: $10,000-$250,000 per violation; (4) Willful neglect, not corrected: $50,000-$1,500,000 per violation. Maximum annual penalty per violation category is $1,500,000.
changes.rationale: Pre-HITECH HIPAA penalties were capped at $25,000 per violation category per year, which was criticized as insufficient deterrent for large covered entities. The tiered penalty structure was created by HITECH and finalized in the 2013 Omnibus Rule to scale penalties to the severity and culpability of violations.

changes.title: Marketing — Prior Authorization Required for Most PHI-Based Marketing
changes.area: 164.514 - Other Requirements Relating to Uses and Disclosures
changes.oldText: A covered entity may use or disclose protected health information for marketing purposes if it obtains an authorization that complies with 164.508, except that no authorization is required for face-to-face marketing communications with an individual or for promotional gifts of nominal value.
changes.newText: A covered entity must obtain a valid authorization before using or disclosing PHI for marketing purposes. An authorization is required for all treatment and health care operations communications where a covered entity receives financial remuneration for making the communication. Subsidized treatment communications (where the third party pays for the communication promoting their products/services) now require authorization. Refill reminders and communications about currently prescribed drugs or biologics are exempt only when financial remuneration received is reasonably related to the cost of making the communication.
changes.rationale: Before the Omnibus Rule, covered entities could receive payment from pharmaceutical companies and others to send marketing materials to patients without obtaining authorization, as long as the communication was about treatment. The 2013 rule closed this loophole by requiring authorization for any subsidized marketing communication.

changes.title: Sale of PHI — Prohibited Without Individual Authorization
changes.area: 164.502 - Uses and Disclosures of Protected Health Information
changes.newText: A covered entity or business associate may not sell protected health information (directly or indirectly receive remuneration in exchange for PHI) without obtaining a valid HIPAA authorization. The authorization must specifically state that the disclosure will result in remuneration to the covered entity. Exceptions include disclosures for public health, research (where remuneration covers costs of preparation only), treatment and payment, health care operations, sale of the covered entity's practice, required by law, and business associates receiving payment in exchange for services not constituting PHI sale.
changes.rationale: Congress determined in HITECH that existing HIPAA regulations did not sufficiently restrict the sale of PHI. The Omnibus Rule implemented HITECH's prohibition on PHI sale without patient authorization, addressing concerns about covered entities monetizing patient data through data brokers and health information exchanges without patients' knowledge.

changes.title: Minimum Necessary — Strengthened and Applied to Business Associates
changes.area: 164.502 - Uses and Disclosures of Protected Health Information
changes.oldText: When using or disclosing protected health information or when requesting protected health information from another covered entity or business associate, a covered entity or business associate must make reasonable efforts to limit protected health information to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose of the use, disclosure, or request.
changes.newText: When using, disclosing, or requesting protected health information, covered entities and business associates must make reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the minimum necessary. The minimum necessary standard now applies to all disclosures including to business associates (previously had broader exceptions). Covered entities must have policies identifying categories of persons who need access to PHI and limiting their access to the minimum necessary for their role. The 'minimum necessary' for de-identified data requests requires documenting why identifiers cannot be removed.
changes.rationale: The Omnibus Rule clarified that minimum necessary applies to all BA disclosures and strengthened requirements for policies defining access limitations. The 2009 HITECH Act had directed HHS to issue guidance on minimum necessary which was incorporated into the 2013 rule.

changes.title: Right of Access — Individuals May Request Electronic PHI in Requested Format
changes.area: 164.524 - Access of Individuals to Protected Health Information
changes.oldText: A covered entity must permit an individual to inspect and copy protected health information about the individual in a designated record set. The covered entity must provide the access as requested by the individual, including in the form and format requested by the individual if it is readily producible in such form and format.
changes.newText: A covered entity must provide individuals access to their PHI in designated record sets. If the PHI is maintained in an electronic health record or other electronic format, the individual has the right to obtain a copy in electronic format, and the covered entity must provide access in the electronic form and format requested if readily producible, or in a readable electronic form and format if not. Individuals have the right to direct the covered entity to transmit a copy of their PHI to a designated person or entity of their choice. The fee for providing PHI is limited to the reasonable cost-based fee including labor, supplies, and postage.
changes.rationale: The Omnibus Rule implemented HITECH's requirement that individuals be able to access their PHI in electronic format and have it transmitted to third parties (e.g., personal health record apps, other providers). This underpins health information exchange and patient engagement initiatives.

changes.title: Subcontractor Business Associates — Must Have BAAs with Their Own Subcontractors
changes.oldText: A covered entity must have a business associate agreement with each business associate that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI on the covered entity's behalf.
changes.newText: A covered entity must have a business associate agreement with each business associate. Business associates must also obtain satisfactory assurances from their subcontractors (business associates of business associates) that they will safeguard PHI appropriately, and must enter into written agreements with their subcontractors that meet the same BAA requirements. The chain of accountability extends downstream to all entities handling PHI.
changes.rationale: Cloud computing and outsourcing created complex chains of PHI handling where direct BAs subcontracted work to other vendors who also handled PHI. The 2013 Omnibus Rule explicitly required BAAs through the entire subcontractor chain, addressing a gap that allowed PHI to flow to parties without any contractual security obligations.

changes.title: Research — Compound Authorizations and Future Research Use Permitted
changes.oldText: Authorization for research must specifically identify the research for which PHI will be used or disclosed. Authorizations for different research studies or purposes must be separated into distinct authorization documents.
changes.newText: Authorization for use or disclosure of PHI for research may be combined with other research-related documents (consent, authorization for future research use). Covered entities may obtain compound authorizations combining authorization for current research with authorization for future unspecified research where the PHI will be maintained in a research database or biospecimen repository, provided the compound authorization clearly distinguishes the two. The authorization must specifically identify the research and describe the PHI to be used.
changes.rationale: Biobanks, genomic research repositories, and longitudinal studies require authorization for future use of samples and data that may not be specified at the time of collection. The 2013 rule relaxed the prior prohibition on compound authorizations to enable important research while maintaining authorization requirements.

changes.title: Security Standards — Genetic Information Protected as PHI, Not Health Information
changes.oldText: The Security Rule applies to electronic protected health information, including demographic information collected from an individual, relating to past, present, or future physical or mental health or condition.
changes.newText: Genetic information is PHI and may not be used or disclosed for underwriting purposes by health plans (other than long-term care plans). The definition of PHI is clarified to explicitly include genetic information under GINA (Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act) alignment. Health plans are prohibited from using or disclosing genetic information for eligibility determinations, premium computation, or any other underwriting purpose.
changes.rationale: The Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA) of 2008 prohibited health plan discrimination based on genetic information. The 2013 Omnibus Rule aligned HIPAA regulations with GINA by explicitly prohibiting health plans from using genetic information as PHI for underwriting, closing a gap in patient protection.

changes.title: Decedent PHI — Protection Extended for 50 Years Post-Death
changes.newText: A covered entity must comply with requirements of this subpart with respect to the protected health information of a deceased individual for a period of 50 years following the death of the individual. After this period, the HIPAA protections no longer apply to the individual's PHI. Prior to the Omnibus Rule, HIPAA protections for deceased individuals were undefined.
changes.rationale: The previous HIPAA regulations did not specify when protections for deceased individuals' PHI expired. The 2013 Omnibus Rule created a definitive 50-year post-death protection period, providing legal clarity for research and genealogy while limiting perpetual protection burdens on covered entities.

changes.title: De-identification — Expert Determination Method Formalized
changes.area: 164.514 - Other Requirements Relating to Uses and Disclosures
changes.newText: Information is de-identified if: (1) Safe Harbor method: 18 specified identifiers are removed and the covered entity has no actual knowledge that the remaining information could be used to identify an individual; OR (2) Expert Determination method: a person with statistical expertise applies generally accepted statistical and scientific principles and methods and determines that the risk of identifying any individual is very small. The expert must document the methods applied and results. The 2013 rule provided additional guidance on re-identification risk assessment for the Expert Determination method.
changes.rationale: The Expert Determination method for de-identification existed in prior HIPAA regulations but lacked specificity on what 'very small' risk meant. The 2013 Omnibus Rule provided additional guidance and required documentation of the expert's analysis to enable OCR enforcement of the standard.

changes.title: Encryption and Decryption — Guidance Clarified as Addressable Implementation Specification
changes.oldText: Implement a mechanism to encrypt and decrypt electronic protected health information. [Addressable]
changes.newText: Implement a mechanism to encrypt and decrypt electronic protected health information. [Addressable] Guidance: Covered entities must assess whether encryption is a reasonable and appropriate safeguard for their environment. Encryption of ePHI at rest and in transit is strongly encouraged. ePHI that is encrypted using methods specified in HHS Guidance (NIST-approved algorithms) is considered 'unsecured PHI' only if the encryption key is not compromised. Encrypted ePHI is not subject to breach notification requirements. The 2013 Omnibus Rule added that covered entities and BAs must document their encryption decisions and must implement encryption unless they determine it is not reasonable and appropriate, documenting the alternative equivalent safeguards in place.
changes.rationale: Encryption remained 'addressable' (not mandatory) under the Omnibus Rule, but OCR issued supplementary guidance emphasizing that failure to encrypt without documented justification and alternative safeguards would be considered willful neglect. Practically, encrypted data is 'safe harbor' from breach notification, creating a strong incentive for encryption.

changes.title: Right to Restrict PHI Disclosures to Health Plans for Self-Pay Services
changes.oldText: A covered entity must permit an individual to request that the covered entity restrict uses or disclosures of protected health information about the individual for treatment, payment, or health care operations, and must agree to restrictions requested by an individual to the extent that such restriction would bind the entity.
changes.newText: A covered entity must agree to an individual's request to restrict disclosure of PHI to a health plan where the PHI pertains solely to a health care item or service for which the individual or the individual's representative has paid the covered entity out of pocket in full. This restriction to health plans for self-pay items is mandatory (not discretionary). The covered entity may not override this restriction even if it believes disclosure would be beneficial to the individual's care.
changes.rationale: Patients sometimes pay out-of-pocket for sensitive health services (mental health, substance abuse, reproductive health) to avoid disclosure to their health plan or employer-sponsored insurance. The Omnibus Rule created a mandatory right to restrict disclosure to health plans for fully self-paid services, protecting patient privacy in these sensitive cases.

highlights: Encryption of ePHI at rest is now required - the 'addressable' designation has been removed
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) required for all access to systems containing ePHI
Covered entities must maintain a written technology asset inventory and network map
Risk analysis now requires specific inputs including vulnerability scan results and asset inventories
Vulnerability scanning required every 6 months; penetration testing required annually
Business associates must now verify their own compliance annually and report to covered entities
Access to ePHI systems must be revoked within 24 hours of workforce termination
Audit logs must be retained for a minimum of 12 months

changes.title: Technology Asset Inventory - New Required Implementation Specification
changes.newText: Covered entities and business associates must create and maintain a written inventory of all technology assets in their environment, including hardware, software, and electronic media that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI. The inventory must be reviewed and updated at least annually and whenever a relevant change occurs.
changes.rationale: HHS found during audits that organizations frequently lacked awareness of all systems that touched ePHI, making it impossible to conduct an accurate risk analysis. A mandatory asset inventory is foundational to all other security controls.

changes.title: Network Map - New Required Implementation Specification
changes.newText: Covered entities and business associates must create and maintain a written network map that illustrates the movement of ePHI throughout their electronic information systems. The network map must be reviewed and updated at least annually and whenever a relevant change occurs to the environment.
changes.rationale: A network map showing data flows is required to perform an accurate risk analysis of ePHI. Without understanding where ePHI travels, organizations cannot identify vulnerabilities in transmission paths.

changes.title: Risk Analysis - Significantly Strengthened with Specific Required Inputs
changes.oldText: Conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of the potential risks and vulnerabilities to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of electronic protected health information held by the covered entity.
changes.newText: Conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of the potential risks and vulnerabilities to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI held by the covered entity or business associate. The risk analysis must be based on the technology asset inventory, the network map, reasonably anticipated threats to ePHI, and the results of vulnerability scans. The risk analysis must be reviewed and updated at least once every 12 months, and whenever a relevant change occurs.
changes.rationale: Audits revealed that many covered entities performed incomplete or superficial risk analyses. The 2025 rule adds specific mandatory inputs (asset inventory, network map, vulnerability scan results) and requires annual reviews.

changes.title: Contingency Plan - Annual Testing of Backup Restore Required
changes.newText: Covered entities and business associates must test the ability to restore ePHI from backup at least once every 12 months. Documentation of the test, including the date, scope, results, and any corrective actions taken, must be retained for 6 years.
changes.rationale: Organizations frequently maintained backups that had never been tested for restoration. The 2025 rule requires documented annual testing to ensure backup mechanisms will actually work during a ransomware attack or system failure.

changes.title: Vulnerability Management - New Standard with 6-Month Scan Cycle and Annual Pen Testing
changes.newText: Covered entities and business associates must implement a vulnerability management process that includes: (1) vulnerability scanning of all relevant electronic information systems at least every 6 months and following significant environmental changes; (2) penetration testing of all relevant electronic information systems at least once every 12 months; (3) timely remediation of identified vulnerabilities based on risk; and (4) documentation of scan results and remediation actions retained for 6 years.
changes.rationale: The previous Security Rule had no explicit vulnerability scanning or penetration testing requirement. Given the surge in healthcare cyberattacks, HHS determined that proactive identification and remediation of vulnerabilities is now essential.

changes.title: Security Incident Procedures - Annual Incident Response Testing Added
changes.oldText: Implement policies and procedures to address security incidents. Identify and respond to suspected or known security incidents; mitigate, to the extent practicable, harmful effects of security incidents that are known to the covered entity; and document security incidents and their outcomes.
changes.newText: Implement policies and procedures to address security incidents. Identify and respond to suspected or known security incidents; mitigate, to the extent practicable, harmful effects of security incidents that are known to the covered entity; and document security incidents and their outcomes. Covered entities and business associates must test their security incident response plan at least once every 12 months through a tabletop exercise, simulation, or equivalent activity.
changes.rationale: Untested incident response plans frequently fail during actual incidents. Annual tabletop exercises ensure staff understand their roles and that contact lists, escalation paths, and notification procedures are current.

changes.title: Termination Procedures - 24-Hour Access Revocation Requirement
changes.oldText: Implement procedures for terminating access to electronic protected health information when the employment of, or other arrangement with, a workforce member ends or as required by determinations made as specified in paragraph (a)(3)(ii)(B) of this section.
changes.newText: Implement procedures for terminating access to ePHI when the employment of, or other arrangement with, a workforce member ends or as required by risk determinations. Access to all relevant electronic information systems must be revoked within 24 hours of termination. For high-privilege accounts (system administrators, security personnel), access must be revoked immediately upon notification of termination.
changes.rationale: Delays in revoking access after termination are a leading cause of insider-threat incidents and data breaches. The 24-hour requirement and immediate revocation for privileged accounts establish clear, enforceable timelines.

changes.title: Encryption - Now Required (Not Addressable) at Rest and in Transit
changes.oldText: Implement a mechanism to encrypt and decrypt electronic protected health information. [Addressable - may use alternative equivalent measures]
changes.newText: Implement encryption to protect electronic protected health information at rest and in transit. Encryption of ePHI is a required implementation specification. Covered entities and business associates must use current valid encryption as defined by NIST - for data at rest, this means AES-128 or higher; for data in transit, TLS 1.2 or higher. The use of alternative equivalent measures in lieu of encryption is no longer permitted.
changes.rationale: The 'addressable' designation for encryption led many covered entities to forgo encryption entirely. Given that unencrypted ePHI on stolen devices is a leading breach category, HHS eliminated the alternative measures option and made encryption mandatory.

changes.title: Multi-Factor Authentication - New Required Implementation Specification
changes.newText: Covered entities and business associates must implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all workforce members accessing relevant electronic information systems that contain ePHI. MFA must require at least two of the following authentication factors: something the user knows (e.g., password), something the user has (e.g., hardware token, authenticator app), or something the user is (e.g., biometric). Phishing-resistant MFA (e.g., FIDO2/WebAuthn) is strongly recommended for privileged accounts and remote access.
changes.rationale: Password-only authentication is insufficient against credential-based attacks, which account for the majority of healthcare data breaches. MFA is widely recognized as one of the highest-impact controls for preventing unauthorized access.

changes.title: Audit Controls - 12-Month Log Retention Now Required
changes.oldText: Implement hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems that contain or use electronic protected health information.
changes.newText: Implement hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems that contain or use ePHI. Audit logs must capture at minimum: successful and failed login attempts, data access and modification events, and system configuration changes. Audit logs must be retained for a minimum of 12 months and protected from unauthorized modification or deletion.
changes.rationale: Without defined minimum retention periods and log scope requirements, many covered entities maintained insufficient audit trails to support breach investigations. The 12-month minimum retention aligns with breach notification and investigation timelines.

changes.title: Business Associate Contracts - Annual Compliance Verification Now Required
changes.oldText: The contract must provide that the business associate will report to the covered entity any security incident of which it becomes aware, including breaches of unsecured protected health information as required by section 164.410.
changes.newText: Business associate agreements must now include provisions requiring: (1) the business associate to implement the security requirements of 45 CFR Part 164 Subpart C; (2) the business associate to provide written attestation of compliance to the covered entity at least annually; (3) the business associate to notify the covered entity of any material changes to their security posture within 30 days; and (4) the covered entity to verify business associate compliance at least annually.
changes.rationale: Business associates are involved in the majority of large healthcare breaches. Requiring annual compliance attestation and active verification by covered entities strengthens oversight of the supply chain handling ePHI.

changes.title: Addressable vs. Required Designation - Framework Restructured
changes.oldText: Implementation specifications are either required or addressable. If an implementation specification is described as 'required,' the covered entity must implement the specification. If it is described as 'addressable,' the covered entity must assess whether the implementation specification is a reasonable and appropriate safeguard.
changes.newText: Implementation specifications are required. The 2025 Final Rule eliminates the 'addressable' category that allowed covered entities to substitute alternative measures for specific technical controls. All implementation specifications in 45 CFR Part 164 Subpart C are now required, and covered entities may not substitute alternative equivalent measures. Scalability is maintained through risk analysis: the specific technologies and methods used to implement required controls must be appropriate to the size, complexity, and capabilities of the covered entity.
changes.rationale: The 'addressable' framework was widely misunderstood and abused. Many organizations interpreted 'addressable' as optional, leading to inadequate security. The 2025 rule replaces addressable designations with a single required standard while preserving flexibility in implementation methods.

changes.title: Security Awareness Training - Annual Phishing Simulation Required
changes.newText: Security awareness training programs must include anti-phishing training and testing. Covered entities and business associates must conduct simulated phishing exercises at least annually for all workforce members with access to ePHI systems. Workforce members who fail phishing simulations must receive targeted remedial training. Training completion must be documented and records retained for 6 years.
changes.rationale: Phishing remains the leading attack vector in healthcare breaches. Awareness training without practical testing is insufficient - simulated phishing exercises identify vulnerable individuals and reinforce learned behaviors.

changes.title: Documentation Retention - Annual Policy Review Now Required
changes.area: 164.316 - Policies and Procedures and Documentation Requirements
changes.oldText: Retain the documentation required by paragraph (b)(1) of this section for 6 years from the date of its creation or the date when it last was in effect, whichever is later.
changes.newText: Retain documentation required under this Subpart for 6 years from the date of creation or last effective date, whichever is later. Policies and procedures must be reviewed by the security official at least once every 12 months and updated as needed to reflect changes in the environment, threats, or workforce. Review must be documented with date, reviewer name, and summary of any changes made or rationale for no changes.
changes.rationale: Security policies that are not regularly reviewed become outdated and fail to reflect the current threat environment. Annual mandatory review ensures documentation stays current.

changes.title: Assigned Security Responsibility - Security Officer Must Report to Senior Leadership
changes.oldText: Identify the security official who is responsible for the development and implementation of the policies and procedures required by this subpart for the entity.
changes.newText: Identify and designate a security official who is responsible for the development, implementation, and oversight of the policies and procedures required by this subpart. The security official must have sufficient authority, resources, and expertise to carry out their responsibilities. For covered entities with 25 or more workforce members, the security official must report directly to senior leadership and provide a written security program status report at least annually.
changes.rationale: Security officials buried in IT departments without access to senior leadership cannot effectively drive security improvements. Requiring direct reporting lines ensures security receives appropriate organizational attention and resources.

changes.title: Access Establishment and Modification - Role-Based Access Control Required
changes.oldText: Implement policies and procedures that, based upon the entity's access authorization policies, establish, document, review, and modify a user's right of access to a workstation, transaction, program, or process.
changes.newText: Implement role-based or attribute-based access control policies that grant workforce members access to ePHI systems based on minimum necessary principles. User access rights must be reviewed at least once every 12 months (quarterly for privileged accounts) and modified promptly when a workforce member's role changes. Access recertification reviews must be documented and records retained for 6 years.
changes.rationale: The principle of least privilege requires systematic access reviews. Many breach investigations revealed that users retained access to ePHI systems long after their need for that access had ended.
